text:
Abstract

The foreign policy opinions of Americans have relatively stable and enduring content, rather than a transitory character. A great deal of speculation has occurred in the literature regarding the potential impact of religious beliefs on foreign policy attitudes, yet very little systematic research exists. This paper addresses this omission by combining the 2012 Chicago Council on Global Affairs public opinion survey with the Wittkopf foreign policy worldview typology in order to locate specific American religious groups along its militant internationalism (MI) and cooperative internationalism (CI) dimensions.

The study found that there are clear religious divisions within American society. Evangelical Protestants and other religious traditionalists in other faiths are the main adherents of militant internationalism, an ideology that supports the use of force and unilateral action to defend a country's interests. At the same time, ethnoreligious minorities, religious modernists and the religiously unaffiliated are more likely to hold cooperative internationalism, an ideology that supports diplomacy and multilateralism. The study explains that ethnic and religious traditions, as well as the culture war divides that have developed within some faiths, are important in shaping Americans' views on their country's role in the world. The study concludes that Americans' religious beliefs are important for foreign policy leaders to keep in mind when considering domestic constraints and opportunities for action abroad.
